entry.title: Best Practices Pipeline for Cloud Integration: Naming Conventions
entry.content:
Co-authored by Slava Kuzyakin.

If you like to run your integration scenarios on the Pipeline for Cloud Integration, you need to create your scenario-specific integration flows as well as various entries in the Partner Directory. Furthermore, you have some flexibility in configuring and setting up the pipeline(s) and pipeline steps, i.e., the integration packages and generic integration flows that are provided on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub. Before you start working with the Pipeline, it's worthwhile to spend some time to define your own naming conventions which allow you to setup your integration scenarios and the pipelines in a common way.

This blog post is intended to help you defining your own naming conventions. They are not binding, they are rather meant as a recommendation. At the end, naming conventions pretty much depend on your own requirements and policies and hence can be applied in different ways. However, the guidelines provided here can be seen as a good starting point and, if necessary, can be extended or modified to your needs.

Partner Directory entries

In the Partner Directory, you have the option to create partner IDs for different purposes:

  • A Configuration Scenario defines how an integration scenario is configured and how a message behaves, e.g., whether or not an inbound conversion is needed, what xpath conditions should be carried out in a content based router pattern, how many retries should be done in case of a message failure before the message is sent to a dead letter queue, etc.
  • A System Alias represents your communication component, whether sender or receiver, within your pipeline. It is independent of your system landscape.
  • A Business Component is a communication component which name is independent of the system landscape, e.g., a communication component representing a file share.
  • A Business System is a communication component which name depends on the system landscape like for scenarios that are connected via XI or IDoc protocol where the system name on the DEV landscape differs from the system name on the PROD landscape. A business system is mapped to a system alias via an alternative partner in the Partner Directory entry of the system alias.
  • For IDocs, we have lately introduced the partner ID type representing a Party Alias. This can be used for partners of type not equal LS where we map the IDoc control record fields that describe the partner to a party alias.

Naming conventions:

  • Use a prefix for your configuration scenario names like CS_
  • We would treat system alias and business components the same, so that you have the option to define a business component as business system at a later point of time with no need to change the configuration scenario if you decide otherwise. So, here you can use the same prefix BC_
  • For the business systems, you have to stick to the names of your existing systems in your landscape, so here you follow your own naming conventions anyway. Otherwise, you may use the prefix BS_
  • For the party alias, you may use the prefix PRTY_

Scenario-specific integration flows

For the scenario-specific integration flows like for inbound processing, inbound conversion, outbound processing, various custom exits, etc., you can actually follow the same naming convention that we apply when generating those integration flows using the migration tool. If you use the migration tool, right now you need to stick to our naming convention anyway. Note, this will change soon, there are plans to make the proposed integration flow names in the migration tool editable.

In the migration tool, the following naming conventions apply in general:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP<pipeline step number>_<pipeline step description>[_<party name>][_<system name>][_IFIndex_<index number>]
  • ProcessDirect endpoint if applicable: /<scenario name>/pip<pipeline step number>[/<party name>][/<system name>][/IFIndex<index number>]

The <scenario name> is the name of your configuration scenario as defined in the Partner Directory.

PIP stands for Process Integration Pipeline. You may omit it and just have the pipeline step number on its own.

The <pipeline step number> corresponds to the sequence of the pipeline steps that a message is processed within the pipeline although not always all pipeline steps are carried out.

The <pipeline step description> shortly describes the pipeline step. You may omit it if you like to shorten the name.

Depending on the pipeline step, you need to add a system and optionally a party if you communicate to a business partner. The <system name> can be either a sender system or a receiver system depending on the context.

The <index number> of the receiver interface applies to the scenario-specific outbound processing flow only. if you have multiple interfaces for the same receiver, the index number is incremented, see below.

Scenario-specific inbound processing

So, for the scenario-specific inbound processing flow, the following applies:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP01_InboundProcessing

In case of a sender wildcard scenario where multiple sender systems reuse the same configuration scenario, the following applies:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP01_InboundProcessing[_<party name>]_<system name>

Example: CS_Scenario_A01_PIP01_InboundProcessing_BC_System_ABC (for a system without party)

Scenario-specific inbound conversion

For the scenario-specific inbound conversion flow, the following applies:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP03_InboundConversion
  • ProcessDirect endpoint: /<scenario name>/pip03

In case of a sender wildcard scenario, each sender may have a different inbound conversion, so we need an own integration flow for each sender system for which an inbound conversion should be carried out:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP03_InboundConversion[_<party name>]_<system name>
  • ProcessDirect endpoint: /<scenario name>/pip03[/<party name>]/<system name>

Example: CS_Scenario_A01_PIP03_InboundConversion_BC_System_ABC with endpoint /CS_Scenario_A01/pip03/BC_System_ABC

Scenario-specific outbound processing

For each receiver and corresponding receiver interface, an own scenario-specific outbound processing flow is generated. If a receiver has multiple receiver interfaces, an interface index number is incremented. We do not use the receiver interface name here because it may not be ambiguous, you may have xpath conditions for determining the interfaces with the same receiver interface name but different mappings for instance.

So, here the following applies:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP07_OutboundProcessing[_<party name>]_<system name>_IFIndex_<index number>
  • ProcessDirect endpoint: /<scenario name>/pip07[/<party name>]/<system name>/IFIndex<index number>

Example: CS_Scenario_A01_PIP07_OutboundConversion_BC_System_XYZ_IFIndex_1 with endpoint /CS_Scenario_A01/pip07/BC_System_XYZ/IFIndex1

Custom receiver determination

In case of extended receiver determination, where the receiver is determined via a message mapping, the message mapping can be reused and a custom receiver determination flow is generated instead of an XSLT.

So, here the following applies:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP04_ReceiverDetermination
  • ProcessDirect endpoint: /<scenario name>/pip04/customXRD

So, actually here the endpoint does not strictly follow the overall rule because a suffix is added indicating that the end point relates to a custom receiver determination.

Creating integration flows from templates

When you manually create the scenario-specific flows, you can apply the same or similar naming conventions like we do in the migration tool. You do not need to create the integration flows from scratch, we actually strongly recommend to copy the provided template flow from the Cloud Integration Pipeline - Templates package. This saves you a lot of effort and it ensures that the allowed header list and the exception subprocess is already properly defined.

Note: Each template comes with a description briefly describing what the template is good for. If you copy the template to your own integration flow, the description of the template is kept. So, you may need to change the description in the copied integration flow's metadata.

Setting up your pipeline(s)

A couple of versions ago, we have split up the provided integration flows in four packages to be more flexible in setting up your pipeline, see New integration packages for the Pipeline for Cloud Integration. We distinguish the following use cases:

  • You like to setup multiple pipelines to distribute your messages across multiple pipelines or to have dedicated pipelines in a shared tenant environment
  • You like to use a dedicated JMS queue for a particular receiver system
  • For sending IDoc or XI messages to Cloud Integration, you may setup dedicated generic IDoc/XI inbound processing flows for each sender system to be able for instance to define a sender-specific user role or to stop incoming messages for a specific sender system
  • For sending IDoc or XI messages from Cloud Integration to the backend system, you may setup dedicated generic IDoc/XI outbound processing flows acting as reusable channels

To setup your pipeline supporting those uses cases, you have to copy the respective packages from the SAP Business Accelerator Hub (actually from the Discovery area in your tenant) to the Design area of your tenant. This ensures that the copied packages are not cut off from future updates, see Updates for SAP's Integration Packages. When you copy those provided packages, you are told that the package already exists and hence to avoid overwriting the existing packages you must add a suffix to the copied package. This suffix is then also added to the ID of the integration flows within the copied packages.

Additional pipeline

If you setup an additional pipeline, you need to copy the three packages Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound, Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Pipelines and Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Outbound. Once you have copied the packages, you need to configure the pipeline prefix (default PIP) of the generic integration flows. For the async pipeline steps, the pipeline prefix determines the JMS queue names. For the sync pipeline, it determines the ProcessDirect endpoint of the sync pipeline step.

So, best practice is to use the same or at least a similar name for both the package suffix and the pipeline prefix. Although you need to be aware of the following restrictions and hence may have to adapt the names accordingly.

  • The package suffix supports the following characters: [A-Za-z0-9.]
  • The JMS queue supports the following characters: [A-Za-z0-9_]
  • In total, the JMS queue name must not exceed 80 characters

Examples:

  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Pipelines.PIPXL with pipeline prefix PIPXL
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Pipelines.ORG.ABC with pipeline prefix ORG_ABC

Dedicated inbound/outbound channel

To setup a dedicated inbound channel, you need to copy the package Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound, for a dedicated outbound channel it's the package Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Outbound.

As suffix of the copied package, you can use the business system alias. You may also add a keyword like BC indicating that this package relates to a business component. If you like to define the inbound/outbound channel for a specific pipeline other than the default pipeline, then you can also combine the channel naming with the pipeline naming as described above.

For the use case of a receiver-specific queues, in the Pipeline v2 Generic Step06 - Outbound Processing flow, you actually have the option to define both a pipeline JMS queue prefix and a pipeline JMS queue suffix. Prefix, the fixed queue number Q04 and the suffix will be concatenated building the JMS queue name. Here, the suffix would also be the business system alias taking into account the character restrictions as mentioned above.

Examples:

  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound.BC.UVW100 for business component UVW100
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound.PIPXL.BC.XYZ200 for business component XYZ200 on pipeline PIPXL
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Outbound.BC.ABC.105 with pipeline JMS queue suffix ABC_105 (queue name PIPQ04ABC_105)

Hope those guidelines help you as starting point to define your own naming conventions. If you have feedback, let us know, please leave a comment at the bottom of the blog post.

entry.title: Re: How to strikeout the Po line item in adobe forms
entry.content: You can wrap the value in <strike>...</strike> dynamically in SAP Adobe Forms by combining: An ABAP flag (LOEKZ = 'L'), A Rich Text field in Adobe LiveCycle. A small JavaScript snippet that inserts the <strike> tag only when needed ABAP: Pass a deletion flag item-del_flag = xsdbool( ekpo-loekz = 'L' ). Adobe Form: Add Dynamic Script Go to the Layout Ready event of the text field. Language: JavaScript if ($record.del_flag == "X") { this.rawValue = "<strike>" + $record.item_text.value + "</strike>"; } else { this.rawValue = $record.item_text.value; }

entry.title: Re: Warehouse number not appearing in Control Cycle (LPK3)
entry.content:
I had the same issue and what was missing is the Business System maintenance:

SCM Extendend Warehouse Management - Extended Warehouse Management - Interfaces - ERP Integration - General Settings - Define Own Business System / Define Business System

We had the Business System defined but the Own Business System was not maintained and the system is looking for this data in the LPK1 program to validate the connection between the EWM Warehouse and IM.

entry.title: Re: How to configure automatic account determination more...
entry.content:
Dear @xinran_hua01ec8,

In Public Cloud there is currently no standard Excel upload function to mass-maintain OBYC / Automatic Account Determination entries. The supported configuration object is SSCUI 100297 Automatic Account Determination. SAP provides an Excel export option for review and documentation purposes but not an Excel import/upload to create or update the configuration directly

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003378370

https://me.sap.com/notes/0002987771

The practical approach is to prepare and validate the mapping in Excel then maintain the entries in SSCUI 100297 and transport the configuration through the standard Public Cloud configuration transport process. If this is a recurring large-volume requirement, it should be treated as a product gap / improvement request rather than a standard configurable feature. The formal channel to raise this is the SAP Customer Influence portal where improvement requests for Public Cloud can be submitted and upvoted by the customer community

More details below:

https://influence.sap.com

Summary:

No standard Excel upload to OBYC/SSCUI 100297 in Public Cloud only export

IMPORTANT:

As an alternative to time-consuming manual data entry, I propose using the official SAP bot (automation package 6PX on the BTP platform), which will autonomously and flawlessly upload our account matrix into the system directly from a prepared Excel file

More details below:

https://api.sap.com/package/eu10.frspa.massuploadofbusinessconfigurationforsaps4hanacloud6pxall2/overview

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Offline Mass Update of Purchasing Info Records through Microsoft Excel
entry.content:
Hello,

we do not grant access to the app to all our Purchasers, but rather share templates for creation / change and perform the changes centrally after review.

Challenge here is that there is no differentiation for create and upload in regards to mandatory fields.

When having no download function available to gather the data upfront a simple change of prices or Purchasing Group is almost impossible. Did anybody else face this issue?

Beside this the configuration help is not logically. On Worksheet 2 there is a column for material and description but not for vendor... It would be nice to have the same logic as in ME11 that either the info record number or Material / vendor needs to be entered.

Also why is the info record type description mandatory in the configuration of WS_2?

entry.title: How to Ensure Sales Order Routing is Selected in MTO Scenarios with Mixed Production Versions?
entry.content:
Hello Experts,
 
I'm facing a challenge with Production Version selection in SAP S/4HANA for materials used in both Make-to-Order (MTO) and Variant Configuration (VC) scenarios. The issue arises when the system does not automatically select the Sales Order Routing, even though it correctly selects the Sales Order BOM.
 
Scenario:
•Material is used in both MTO and VC scenarios.
•Two Production Versions are defined:
–PV 0001: Super BOM and Super Routing.
 
Problem:
•During MRP, the system selects PV 0001, applying Sales Order routing and  Super Routing instead of the Sales Order Routing to the planned order.
 
Questions:
1. How can I configure the system to prioritize Sales Order Routing when it exists?
2. Are there any BAdIs or enhancements that can automate this selection?
3. Has anyone faced similar issues, and what solutions have you implemented?
 
Any insights or suggestions would be greatly appreciated.
 
MAN Production Planning (PP) SCM APO Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling (PP/DS)

entry.title: Re: Business Volume Report and Settlement are not in sync
entry.content:
Hi Martina,

If you are using the Business volume profile as VWB2_VBRKVBRP_9, Business volume report should fetch only the paid billing documents. Check the business volume profile assigned to the commission's contract type.

Regards

Hari

entry.title: Help Us Improve Your Learning Experience – Join a 30–60 Minute Interview
entry.content:
🎤 Help Us Improve Your Learning Experience – Join a 30–60 Minute Interview

Hello SAP Cloud ERP Learners,

We invite you to take part in an interview to share your experiences with SAP learning.

Why are we reaching out

Our goal is simple: to better understand your learning requirements and experience.
We want to learn what works well for you, where challenges arise, and how we can continue to improve and evolve our SAP Cloud ERP learning portfolio. Your feedback helps us make learning more relevant, accessible, and impactful for everyone in the SAP ecosystem. 

Who we are looking for

We are looking for volunteers who are willing to share their experiences in a conversation with our team. We’re particularly interested in employees who have started in their roles (consulting, sales, presales, etc.) within the past 3 years and have used/are using SAP’s learning offerings to onboard and upskill. Of course, we also welcome perspectives from more experienced learners.

What to expect

In the interview, we would like to talk about your personal learning experience, expectations, and overall perspective on learning at and about SAP. There are no right or wrong answers; we are interested in your honest and individual point of view. No preparation is required.

What is in it for you

  • An opportunity to share your voice directly with the team shaping SAP’s Cloud ERP product and solution learning
  • Help improve the learning experience for thousands of learners worldwide
  • A chance to reflect on your own learning journey

How to participate

If you are interested, we’d love to hear from you! Please reply to this post or send us a direct message. We will then reach out to schedule a session at a time that works for you.

Thank you for being an important part of our learning community. We truly appreciate your time, insights, and willingness to help us improve.

entry.title: Which SAP skills and modules are most in demand for 2026 projects?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are a growing SAP consulting and staffing company trying to better understand SAP hiring and implementation trends for 2026.

We are seeing increasing demand around:

  • SAP BTP

  • SAP S/4HANA

  • SAP Security/GRC

  • SAP Integration Suite

  • SAP AI and Automation

We would appreciate insights from SAP consultants, implementation partners, recruiters, and hiring managers on the following:

  1. Which SAP skills are currently hardest to hire for?

  2. Which SAP modules are seeing the highest project demand?

  3. Are clients preferring certifications or hands-on implementation experience?

  4. Which SAP technologies should consulting companies invest in for long-term growth?

Looking forward to learning from the SAP Community.

Thank you.

entry.title: The 2026 Developer Insights Survey: The Report
entry.content:
589 Responses from Developers in 44 Countries

All charts can be enlarged for easier review.  Just click on the image to zoom it properly.

Part I: Introduction and General Information

Welcome to the 2026 SAP Developer Insights Survey report. This annual survey targets SAP’s existing external developer audience. It is used to track demographics, usage patterns, and trends within that population. 2026 is the sixth year we have conducted the survey. The survey program is led by the SAP Developer Advocates team. Key support is provided by the SAP Customer, Competitive & Market Insights office. Several other SAP teams provide advice and inputs to survey content.

The 2026 survey was open for six weeks - from February 16th through March 30th.

Using the same pattern as in years past, we solicited responses via social media announcements, SAP's Developer News, and a web-popup on Community web sites. We invited all technical professionals in the SAP ecosystem to participate in the survey.

Each year's survey is composed of a mix of new topic questions and repeat select questions from past years to track trends.

Key Findings from the 2026 Survey

  • AI has Arrived and is Dramatically Changing How Developers Work - Use of AI on production SAP projects continues to increase - up ten percent from last year (32% compared to 21%). This represents a shift from near-zero adoption two years ago to nearly one-third of developers today. The AI section  of our report deserves a close look. We asked many new questions that explore the sentiment of Developers who are actively using these tools. The sentiments expressed are quite positive and point to fundamental changes in how software is developed.

  • AI is a Learning Tool, too - AI isn't just for coding; it is a learning tool too. 70% report using AI to explain something or to help them learn new concepts; 30% report doing this daily 

  • Developer Certifications are not ubiquitous - Over one third of respondents have received no certifications  in the past four years. While we did not explore the reasons behind this in the survey, we did ask about other "go to" resources preferred by respondents.

These topics and others will be covered in more detail in the rest of the report. An index of topics is available on the right hand margin for quick navigation.

General Topics

We included a number of general questions in the survey covering such topics as age, work location and status, as well as role.

Employment Status and Relation to SAP

Employment
"What is your current employment status?"  

 

SAP Developer Community Composition
"Which choice best describes your relation to SAP?" 
We have seen no significant change in these breakdowns from year to year.
 

SAP Developer Community Age Distribution

This shows increases in both the oldest age buckets for 2026. This was accompanied by similar decreases in the middle two age buckets (ranging from 25–44 years of age). The increases in the oldest age buckets and corresponding decreases in the 25–44 range warrant further investigation to understand whether this reflects demographic shifts in hiring or retention.

Workplace and Trends

Office Location
"As of right now, where do you primarily physically work from?"  
 Year-to-year trend in Workplace
 

This shows basically a halt to the trend of employees moving back to conventional offices from home. From a past Developer Survey, 29% of respondents reported they had primarily worked at home prior to the pandemic.

Part II. Narrowing the Response Set

A primary objective of our survey is to build a model of our community's usage patterns. It helps us build more effective programs. With that goal in mind, in the remainder of the report we will narrow the data to filter SAP employees and SAP contractors from the result sets. Unless otherwise indicated, all results below exclude SAP employees and SAP contractors.

Use of Generative AI for Developers

We repeated two questions from last year, looking for a general sense of the effectiveness of AI-assisted development.

 
Current Gen AI Use
"How would you characterize your team’s current use of Generative AI tools or LLMs in your day-to-day work? Consider tools from any vendor or open source. Examples might include Joule, SAP Generative AI Hub, GitHub Copilot, or open source LLMs. (select the most applicable statement)"  
 

Important to our report: for the remainder of the AI section, we only asked these follow-up AI question to respondents who confirmed they, "are actively using Gen AI" today.

 
Gen AI Effectiveness for Developers
"Looking back on the past year, what is your own sense of how the use of Generative AI based tools or LLMs impacted the effectiveness or efficiency of your team?" 
 

While one might say there's an element of Survivor Bias when asking this sentiment question, it is still dramatic to see that three quarters of the respondents agree that there was a "profound positive impact" in using these tools.

 
Using GenAI in Production Applications
"In the past year, did your team use Generative AI to help build a production application? (select the statement that best applies for each use case)"  
Developing and Maintaining Software with Generative AI

For the first time this year, we asked respondents to rate the usefulness of GenAI tools across different phases of the software development lifecycle.

 
Generative AI by Software Development Lifecycle Phases
"Rate the usefulness of Generative AI tools for your team these different phases of software development - how valuable are they today or in the foreseeable future? (select the best response for each phase)" 
 
Somewhat surprisingly, coding ("Implementation") only comes in at fourth on this list, behind Documentation, Code Quality, and (tied with) Testing.
 
GenAI Tools Used for Development
"For the last production project where your team used GenAI tooling for development, which tool(s) did you use?" 
 
Runtime LLM Interactions
"For the same project, did your architecture include runtime interactions with one or more LLMs?" 
 
 
GenAI Agents Used to Write Code
"For that same project, which GenAI tool(s) did you use as Agents to write code or perform other development functions?"  
 

Developer Perceptions of GenAI Tools

We asked developers to rate their agreement with several statements about their experience using Generative AI tools.

 
GenAI Usage in Day-to-Day Work
"I am currently using one or more Generative AI tools in performing my day to day work" 
 
Productivity with GenAI Tools
"I'm able to perform my job more quickly when I use these Generative AI tools."  

New Capabilities with GenAI

"I am now doing things that I never would have been able to do without Generative AI." 
GenAI First-Try Accuracy
"I find that these Generative AI tools often give me the correct outcome on their first try"  
 
Using GenAI for Learning
"How often do you use these tools to explain concepts or even help you learn something that might not be directly related to solving a specific problem?" 

Where are the Developers?

We asked, "Which country are you based in?"

PositionCountryRespondent Count
1India52
2Germany25
3United States of America19
4Switzerland8
5Canada7
6Brazil5
7Turkey5
8Portugal5
9Spain4
10Austria4
11Denmark3
12Pakistan3

 

The country proportions remain consistent with past surveys. SAP region composition varies from year to year.

Development Job Roles

We asked respondents which common development-related roles they perform.

 
"Which roles describe your day-to-day work? (Select all that apply)"

 

Top seven role overlaps in external SAP Developers
 
Respondents often "wear multiple hats" in their job. This question is asked in "select all that apply" form to capture that information. The Venn diagram on the right depicts the relative overlaps of the top seven roles reported by respondents. The area of each overlapping region reflects the count respondents performing the overlapping roles. For instance, all respondents identifying as UX Designers also identify as Developers, while most (but not all) Enterprise Architects also work as Solution Architects.

These patterns of overlap are very similar to those in past years.

It is unsurprising that the "Developer" role leads. Architecture roles as a group have increased each year.

Development Domains

For respondents selecting "Developer" as one of their roles, we were interested in the specific application Domains they work in:

"Which of the following development domains describe your day-to-day work? (select all that apply)"  

Year to year, Integration came in higher than we expected. Mobile web front-end development is more common than native mobile application development. This isn't completely unexpected, but worth noting.

Recent SAP Project Experience?

"Have you developed integrations or extensions for any SAP products or technologies in the past 12 months?"  

Extension and Integration are key scenarios for developers working with SAP applications. We wanted to focus on developers with recent experience in these areas. Roughly three quarters of developers answered "Yes" here.

Popular Programming Languages

"Which programming languages have you used in your development work over the past 12 months? Please select all that apply."  
This was posed as a multiple response question. ABAP dominates, as it has in every year of our survey. JavaScript is very popular as well, which could be attributed to its use in both SAPUI5 and CAP. Java and Python are roughly tied for third place. The relative positions of these top four languages hasn't changed much, year to year.

Popular Programming Frameworks

A few years back, we extended our survey beyond just language use to ask about popular frameworks. We asked respondents who are Developers to pick which common front and back-end frameworks they have worked with in the past year.

Front-Ends

Back-Ends

Application and Business Technology Platform Architectures

SAP applications can be extended using any one of several architectural approaches. These can be grouped into two major classes: on-stack (in-app) and side-by-side (essentially, BTP-based).

We were wondering which approaches are most popular.

 
Popular Extension Architectures
"For the most recent extension project that you were part of, what was the principal runtime architecture of the back-end elements?"  
 

 

We don't distinguish between newer and older SAP applications (e.g., S/4HANA vs. ECC) -- this likely explains the dominance of "on-stack ABAP". Aside from the obvious dominance of ABAP, we can also see that CAP is relatively popular. As with last year's results on this question, we see Low Code products like Build Apps and Build Process Automation are popular -- this question is asking about real production projects, and seeing those two account for roughly 10% of all extension projects highlights the value of these tools.

For the first time, CAP is now on-par with ABAP as a side-by-side extension option: 22% of reported deployed applications were CAP-based (Cloud Foundry, Kyma, and HANA deployments) compared to 21% for BTP ABAP Environment.

ABAP and CAP both have style or language variants in their respective frameworks. ABAP can be programmed as "ABAP for Cloud Development" or "ABAP Standard". CAP is available in JavaScript or Java language variants. Where the respondent selected ABAP or CAP, we asked about the variant used on the project:

ABAP Variants

CAP Variants

In years past, the ABAP breakdown was closer to a 50-50 mix between older, "ABAP Standard" and "ABAP Cloud". This year we see a marked increase for ABAP Cloud (almost 60% total). 
 
CAP/JavaScript is plainly most popular for CAP projects. Consistent with last year, we do have more respondents reporting the use of pure CAP/Java projects, although the percentage is a modest 8 percent.
 
Which Integrated Development Environments are Developer Using
We were curious which Integrated Development Environments are most familiar to our respondents.
 
IDE Familiarity
"Which of these IDEs are are you comfortable working with? (select all that apply)"  

BTP Guidance Framework

Awareness and Use of the BTP Guidance Framework
"The BTP Guidance Framework is intended to help architects, developers, and administrators design, build, and run solutions on the SAP Business Technology Platform. Which statement best described your level of familiarity with this document?"  

 

Nearly half of respondents are still unaware of this document's release. Raising awareness for this and other related BTP framework documents would be a good goal for 2026.

Cloud Insights

We first asked respondents if they used any cloud providers for their projects. For those that answered, "yes", we asked about the use of several major providers, including SAP BTP. We also asked if their use included SAP or non-SAP projects. This information was condensed into a chart depicting the relative use of each cloud provider.

Similar to the earlier questions around BTP Environments, these numbers do not reflect a tally of projects for each platform - instead it reflects developer exposure to each.

 
Using Cloud Providers?
"Are you using any cloud providers for your development projects?" 
Relative Use of Cloud Providers
We asked, "Which cloud providers are you using for development projects?"
 
SAP Business Technology Platform
Microsoft Azure
Amazon Web Services
Google Cloud Platform
Alibaba Cloud

Low Code / No Code Tools

This is the fourth consecutive year in which we've asked questions around Low Code and No Code (LCNC) products. The list was composed of leaders in the then current Gartner LCNC Magic Quadrant Report and select SAP products.

For each product, we asked respondents to select the most applicable category reflecting their awareness or use. These questions were organized as a single large matrix of boxes in Qualtrics - There is no "I am unfamiliar with this product" response. Instead, the respondent simply would skip checking one of the other boxes.

We normalized these data over the reporting years to account for differences in total responses each year. We then tallied production use of each tool in the table below, ordering by use for the current year. Note that respondents may have deployed more that just one product each year, so column totals will not necessarily add up to 100%.

|   Note: Figures for 2024–2025 differ from last year's report due to a correction to the original formula; relative rankings were unaffected.

 

Tool Name 2024% 2025% 2026%
Tool Name 2024% 2025%2026%
SAP Build low code55.061.467.6
Vendor A35.035.741.2
Vendor B20.027.114.7
Vendor Xnana11.8
'na' - not part that year's survey   

SAP Build low code continues to dominate our SAP ecosystem.

Learning and Help Resources

We were interested in preferences in the format or media type of learning resources by respondents.

 
Preferred Learning Resources
"When learning new skills or technical subjects over the past 12 months, what resources did you tend use the most? Please select up to three resources."  
 

These results seem relatively unchanged year-to-year. One point of interest continuing from last year is the large difference in popularity between tutorials and MOOCs (34% versus 8% for 2026). Tutorials tend to be hands-on. They are also broken into substantially smaller time chunks. This reinforces our hypothesis that different content types and required time investment have an impact in some learning scenarios.

On the topic of Help, we shifted in this next question from formats to specific web sites.

 
Top SAP Help Resource Sites
"What is your preferred resource if you need help, or have a challenge with the SAP technology / tool you are working with? "  
We added "Consulting an LLM ..." this year and -- interestingly -- it comes in third place. Although we consider "Help" different than "Learning", this is still an interesting point when it comes to describing LLM's roles in assisting Developers.

SAP Discovery Center Awareness and Use

SAP Discovery Center is a rich resource for helping teams understand how common business use cases map to technical architectures. Other important aspects of SAP BTP use are available there as well. How well know is it outside of SAP and, for folks that do know about it, how often do they use it?

 
BTP Discovery Center
"SAP Discovery Center helps you discover, evaluate, and adopt SAP BTP services and AI features through missions, a detailed service catalog, reference architectures and more. Which of the following statements best describes your level of familiarity with SAP Discovery Center?"  

Technical Certifications

Technical Certifications
"How many technology-related certification programs have you completed within the past four years?"  
 
 

This question is composed to apply to all technology certifications, not just SAP's. But we can see that well over a third (36%) of respondents took no certifications in the past four years. We chose "four years" on purpose. It's designed to be a long period - one that would allow most technical skills to become stale. If the respondent valued these as a means to truly stay current, we reason they'd be likely to re/certify.

For roughly a third of folks, that's not the case.

 
SAP Technical Certifications
"Of these certifications, how many were from SAP?"  
 
This was a follow-up question for those that did take at least one certification. It does appear to indicated that -- for those taking certifications -- that SAP's are among those acquired.

Survey Methodology

This report is based on a Qualtrics web-based survey of 589 respondents from 44 countries. The survey ran for six calendar weeks. Ninety percent of the respondents invested ten minutes or less with the survey.

The survey was promoted via the SAP Community website and the SAP Developer Center, sap.com pop-up intercepts, social media posts.

This Report

This is the fourth year that this report is published as a Docusaurus web application. Docusaurus is a Node-based documentation publishing system. Recharts and venn.js were used to generate custom charts from survey data exported from Qualtrics and anonymized.

Survey results are presented by topic area and are filtered based on question relevance and survey logic. While the survey received 589 total responses, not every respondent saw or answered every question. Some questions were shown only to relevant audiences based on earlier responses, and some allowed multiple selections. As a result, totals and percentages may vary across sections and should be interpreted within the context of each chart. For example, a section focused on AI adoption may reflect only respondents who answered AI-related questions, while Developer Experience and Learning may include broader audiences. This structure helps ensure that each section reflects the most relevant responses, but it also means that sample sizes and percentages can vary throughout the report.

The sections in this report provide a directional view of the SAP developer community in 2026, highlighting key patterns in developer roles, tools, learning habits, and technology adoption. In sections such as Developer Experience, the report focuses on workflows, environments, and friction points in day-to-day development. In Learning, it highlights how developers build skills, with strong reliance on tutorials, documentation, forums, and community resources. For example, the learning section shows that developers most often relied on developer community websites and forums (60%), online tutorials, how-to videos, or webinars (47%), and self-directed learning using documentation and sample code (41%).

Other References

The annual Stack Overflow Developer Survey is an excellent (and no-cost) reference for industry-wide habits of developers.

SlashData's Developer Nation Survey is also a good non-SAP resource.

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA configuration for SAC pull of plan data
entry.content:
Hello Sivaramakrishnan,

Thanks for the summary.

I have one remark on CAPEX:

My previous mentioned "budget" functionality is only available for cost center, project and public sector for which there are corresponding upload templates available:

That means with these “budget” features you can set/lock budgets, and based on that budget availability checks are executed during the posting of actual data. These functionalities make no sense for CAPEX.
Information about this budget availability control and maintaining category for planning is available in the following:
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c56f622a2edf491b9f1b596b55587009/eb9bf27fce10431e830d472402f470d3.html?locale=en-US&version=LATEST 

On your question: if it is permanently ensured that planning data remans disjoint and there are reporting requirements for a consolidated reporting (e.g. P&L), a common planning category should be used. But it makes no difference whether this is a SAP delivered planning category (e.g. PLN) or own created planning categories. The SAP-delivered categories can be used immediately. However, own created planning categories can be used as well, but these must be created correctly (in particular regarding the selected Application Type and Category Usage). All standard reports displaying Periodic Planning data should not be restricted solely to planning category 'PLN'.

I hope this helps.

Kind regards,
Tarkan Citirgi
